title: Cobalt and Copper Hexacyanoferrate Modified Carbon Fiber Microelectrode as an All-Solid Potentiometric Microsensor for Hydrazine
abstract:
Cobalt and Copper Hexacyanoferrate Modi®ed Carbon Fiber
Microelectrode as an All-Solid Potentiometric Microsensor
for Hydrazine
Jian-Wei Mo,+ BozÏidar Ogorevc,*+ Xueji Zhang,+,++ and Boris Pihlar+++
+
Analytical Chemistry Laboratory, National Institute of Chemistry, P.O. Box 3430, 1001 Ljublijana, Slovenia
++
Present address: World Precision Instriments Inc., 175 Sarasota Center Boulevard, Sarasota, Florida 34240-9258, USA
+++ Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Technology, University of Ljubljana, Ljubljana, Slovenia
Received: April 22, 1999
Final version: July 28, 1999
Abstract
This article reports on the novel mixed cobalt and copper hexacyanoferrate (CoCuHCF)-modi®ed carbon ®ber cylinder microelectrode
(CFCME) and its application to potentiometric determination of highly toxic hydrazine. The substrate CFCMEs were fabricated in a
standard manner using carbon ®bers of 7 mm in diameter. The CoCuHCF ®lm was deposited electrochemically by cycling the potential
between 0 and  1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in a solution containing the precursor salts. It exhibited good chemical stability in the pH range from
1 to 9. The effects of the coverage/thickness of the CoCuHCF coating, of the pH of a measurement solution, and of the activation of the
sensor, on the potentiometric response to hydrazine were examined in detail. The potentiometric behavior of CoCuHCF ®lm in the presence
of hydrazine, with the potential (emf ) slope of 7 55 mV/decade, was compared with those of four single metal HCF ®lms (CoHCF,
CuHCF, FeHCF, and NiHCF) prepared in the same way, and the possible role of the CoCuHCF ®lm components is explored and discussed.
The emf versus log CHyd calibration plot was linear over 3 orders of magnitude, from 1.061076 to 1.061073 mol/L, with a correlation
coef®cient of 0.995. The limit of detection was found to be 561077 mol/L. The response times of 10 to 30 s and the temperature
coef®cients of ca ±2.7 mV/C were obtained at various hydrazine concentrations. The repeatability of measurements was very good with the
relative standard deviation ranging from 3 to 4 % (n  10), depending on hydrazine concentration. The selectivity of the reported micro-
sensor was found excellent, except in the case of a few negatively charged interferents for which it was greately improved by an additional
Na®on coating. The useful lifetime of the all-solid microsensor was found to be more than 2 months when stored in air.
Keywords: Hydrazine, Potentiometry, Ion selective electrodes, Modi®ed electrodes, Microelectrodes, Hexacyanoferrate
1. Introduction
Hydrazine is a very toxic compound that may cause cancer
and heritable genetic damage. In an organism it is believed to be
oxidized by peroxidases into reactive intermediates capable of
causing a variety of adverse side effects including DNA harm
[1]. Hydrazine is found naturally in plants and throughout the
environment. However, it is widely used in large quantities by
the military and aerospace industry as a rocket propellant and a
fuel for fuel cells, and is also employed for a diversity of
applications by the chemical, pharmaceutical, and agricultural
industries [2]. Recently, it was found that its derivatives can be
used as inhibitors of some enzymes [3] and HIV integrase, thus
being sought as potential new therapeutics for the treatment of
AIDS [4]. It is therefore obvious that reliable and sensitive
analytical methods for the determination of hydrazine in macro-
as well as in microvolume samples are needed. Various
methods and detection modes have already been reported, e.g.,
those using chemiluminescence [5], spectrophotometry [6], and
coulometric titration [7], as well as different chromatographic
methods [8, 9], but these can be very complex, time consuming
and/or need special equipment, although some of them are very
sensitive.
Due to the large overpotential of hydrazine oxidation at bare
solid electrodes (e.g., over 1 V at the platinum electrode), its
amperometric or voltammetric detection at such electrodes is
rather inconvenient. Many efforts have, therefore, been focused
on applying differently modi®ed electrodes, that recently have
successfully resulted in amperometric [10±13] and potentio-
metric stripping analysis [14] determination of hydrazine. On the
other hand, the few potentiometric methods published, e.g.,
titration of hydrazine with Ce(IV) [15] and the employment of
an iodide ion-selective electrode after reaction of iodine with
hydrazine in CHCl3 [16], were capable of determining hydrazine
only in the 10ÿ3 mol=L range. So far, to our knowledge, there
has been no report of a direct potentiometric sensor for hydra-
zine.
Metal
hexacyanoferrate
(MeHCF)
deposits
as
electrode
modi®ers have been extensively investigated to exploit their
electrocatalytic properties for the determination of various
organic and inorganic species. For example, different MeHCFs
incorporated into carbon paste were examined for their voltam-
metric response to various cations [17, 18], CoHCF ®lm was
used for the amperometric determination of hydrogen peroxide
[19], NiHCF was employed as a biosensor component for the
determination of either ethanol [20] or glucose [21], FeHCF was
used for the catalytic amperometric detection of hydrazine [10]
etc. However, mixed metal HCF mediators seem not yet to have
been explored for analytical purposes.
Beside electrode surface modi®cation, the size and shape of
the signal transducing substrate body may play a signi®cant role
in tailoring a desired magnitude and type of sensor response. As
is well known, owing to their attractive inherent advantages,
microelectrodes have been given great attention, in particular
due to the possibility of their use in microlocation (micro-
pro®ling) and microvolume (single drop) sample measurements
[22±25].
This article reports on a new potentiometric all-solid micro-
sensor for hydrazine produced by modifying a substrate carbon
®ber
cylinder
microelectrode
(CFCME)
with
the
electro-
48

1040±0397/00/0101±0048 $17.50:50=0
 chemically deposited mixed cobalt and copper hexacyanoferrate
(CoCuHCF) thin ®lm. The potentiometric characteristics and
analytical performance of the microsensor were studied in detail.
Special attention was paid to its stability, response time, and
selectivity.
2. Experimental
2.1. Apparatus
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using a modular
electrochemical system Autolab (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The
Netherlands), equipped with PSTAT10 and ECD modules, dri-
ven by GPES software (Eco. Chemie). The three-electrode
con®guration employed in voltammetric studies consisted of a
carbon ®ber cylinder microelectrode (CFCME) modi®ed with a
metal
hexacyanoferrate
(MeHCF),
a
platinum
wire
and
Ag=AgCl=KCl(saturated) as the working, counter, and the
reference electrode, respectively. Measurements of the sensor's
electromotive force (emf) was carried out either with a modular
electrochemical system Autolab (Eco. Chemie) employing the
pX module and the ISEC software (Eco. Chemie) or with a
MA5736 digital pH-mV meter (Iskra, Horjul, Slovenia). The
two-electrode con®guration used in potentiometric studies con-
sisted of a MeHCF-modi®ed CFCME and Ag=AgCl=KCl(satu-
rated) as the indicator and the reference electrode, respectively.
All electrochemical experiments were carried out in a standard
Metrohm-type voltammetric cell at conditioned room tempera-
ture (20+1 C), except for the determination of temperature
coef®cients when a water-thermostated double-wall voltam-
metric cell was employed.
2.2. Chemicals and Solutions
Potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) and hydrazine sulfate were
obtained from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland). Warning: hydrazine
is highly toxic to humans and should be handled with great
care; consult the accompanying Safety Data Sheet. Cobalt(II)
nitrate hexahydrate was obtained from Carlo Erba (Milano,
Italy), copper(II) acetate and iron(III) nitrate nonhydrate from
Kemika (Zagreb, Croatia), and nickel(II) sulfate hexahydrate
from Merck (Germany). 5 % Na®on (Nf) lower aliphatic alcohol
solution was supplied by Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). All
chemicals employed in this work were of analytical-grade purity
and used as received. Water used to prepare all solutions was
®rst deionized and then further puri®ed via a Milli-Q unit
(Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).
Hydrazine standard solutions were prepared daily. A mod-
i®cation solution for applying a mixed cobalt and copper hex-
acyanoferrate (CoCuHCF) coating was composed of 0.1 mol=L
NaNO3 and 1610ÿ4 mol=L K3[Fe(CN)6] aqueous solution
containing 5610ÿ5 mol=L of Co(NO3)2 and 5610ÿ5 mol=L of
Cu(CH3COO)2. Other MeHCFs, i.e., CoHCF, NiHCF, FeHCF
and CuHCF modi®cation solutions were composed of 0.1 mol=L
NaNO3 and a 1610ÿ4 mol=L K3[Fe(CN)6] aqueous solution
containing 1610ÿ4 mol=L of Co(NO3)2, NiSO4, Fe(NO3)3 and
Cu(CH3COO)2, respectively. 0.1 mol=L sodium phosphate buffer
solution (NaPBS) was prepared by mixing aqueous solutions of
sodium dihydrogenphosphate and disodium hydrogenphosphate
to achieve a desired pH.
2.3. Microsensor Preparation
2.3.1. Fabrication of CFCMEs
Glass capillary tubes (Euroglass, Ljubljana, Slovenia) were
pulled with a PP-830 pipette puller (Narishige, Tokyo, Japan), to
form very thin capillaries of ca 10 mm inner diameter. A cleaned
carbon ®ber (7 mm in diameter, Goodfellow, Oxford, UK) was
mounted on the end of a copper wire (about 0.2 mm in diameter)
using high-purity silver paint (SPI Supplies, West Chester, PA,
USA) and inserted into a pulled capillary under an inverted
microscope (M40, Wild, Switzerland), so that about 0.5 mm of
the ®ber was left protruding. The ®ber was sealed into the
capillary mouth by using a nonconducting epoxy resin. Further
details on CFCME fabrication are given elsewhere [25].
2.3.2. Modification of CFCMEs
Prior to modi®cation, a CFCME was pretreated electro-
chemically in a 0.1 mol=L NaNO3 solution by applying a con-
tinuous potential scan between 0.0 and 1.0 V at a scan rate of
100 mV=s
until
a
steady-state
current-voltage
pro®le was
reached. Subsequently, the electrode was thoroughly washed with
water. The CoCuHCF ®lm was electrochemically grown on a
CFCME in the CoCuHCF modi®cation solution (for its com-
position see Sec. 2.2.) by cycling the potential between 0.0 and
1.0 V at a scan rate of 100 mV=s until a desired coverage was
obtained (25 CV runs if not stated otherwise). The CoHCF,
NiHCF, FeHCF and CuHCF ®lms were deposited in the corre-
sponding MeHCF modi®cation solutions applying the electro-
chemical procedure described above. After accomplishing ®lm
preparation, the modi®ed electrode was thoroughly rinsed with
water and stored in air. Prior to each set of measurements, the
modi®ed electrodes were activated in a 0.1 mol=L sodium nitrate
solution by applying CV between 0.0 and 1.0 Vat a scan rate of
100 mV=s for ca. 15 cycles. The ®nal potential of the activation
was 1.0 V if not stated otherwise. Na®on coating was performed
by dipping a CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME 5 times into a 1 % Nf
ethanol solution, using a laboratory made dip-coating device, at 5
to 10 min intervals to allow the Nf ®lm to dry in between.
3. Results and Discussion
3.1. Preparation of the Modi®ed Electrode
The mixed cobalt and copper hexacyanoferrate (CoCuHCF)
®lm was deposited on a CFCME electrochemically by applying
multiscan cyclic voltammetry in a CoCuHCF modi®cation
solution, which is represented in Figure 1. A reversible sym-
metric behavior typical for an adsorbed redox couple can be
depicted with the anodic and cathodic peak potentials of 0.44 and
0.40 V, respectively, and the anodic-to-cathodic peak current ratio
of approximately 1. Furthermore, the peak currents were found to
increase continuously even when the number of CV runs
exceeded 50, indicating a constant increase in the thickness of the
electroactive layer. These recordings thus display a reversible
redox behavior and suggest that the conductivity of the
CoCuHCF ®lm is very good.
To enable a comparison, cyclic voltammograms of CuCoHCF,
CoHCF and CuHCF ®lms in the corresponding modi®cation
solutions, as well as of a bare CFCME in 0.1 mol=L NaNO3
solution containing 1610ÿ4 mol=L K3[Fe(CN)6], were recorded
as shown in Figure 2. While CuHCF ®lm redox behavior is

This article reports on the novel mixed cobalt and copper hexacyanoferrate (CoCuHCF)-modi®ed carbon ®ber cylinder microelectrode
(CFCME) and its application to potentiometric determination of highly toxic hydrazine. The substrate CFCMEs were fabricated in a
standard manner using carbon ®bers of 7 mm in diameter. The CoCuHCF ®lm was deposited electrochemically by cycling the potential

1 to 9. The effects of the coverage/thickness of the CoCuHCF coating, of the pH of a measurement solution, and of the activation of the
sensor, on the potentiometric response to hydrazine were examined in detail. The potentiometric behavior of CoCuHCF ®lm in the presence

The emf versus log CHyd calibration plot was linear over 3 orders of magnitude, from 1.061076 to 1.061073 mol/L, with a correlation
coef®cient of 0.995. The limit of detection was found to be 561077 mol/L. The response times of 10 to 30 s and the temperature

sensor was found excellent, except in the case of a few negatively charged interferents for which it was greately improved by an additional
Na®on coating. The useful lifetime of the all-solid microsensor was found to be more than 2 months when stored in air.

3.1. Preparation of the Modi®ed Electrode

®lm was deposited on a CFCME electrochemically by applying

solution, which is represented in Figure 1. A reversible sym-

To enable a comparison, cyclic voltammograms of CuCoHCF,
CoHCF and CuHCF ®lms in the corresponding modi®cation
solutions, as well as of a bare CFCME in 0.1 mol=L NaNO3
solution containing 1610ÿ4 mol=L K3[Fe(CN)6], were recorded
as shown in Figure 2. While CuHCF ®lm redox behavior is
Co and Cu Hexacyanoferrate Modi®ed Carbon Fiber Microelectrode
notably different in peak potentials and shapes (Fig. 2, curve d),
CoCuHCF and CoHCF ®lms exhibit somehow similar redox
behavior (curves b and c). Yet, it is also evident that the presence
of the copper(II) ions in the modi®cation solution contributed to
the formation of a new ®lm (CoCuHCF) with distinct CV
behavior and properties. For the optimum ®lm response, it was
cyanoferrate(III) and a precursor salt (or their mixture) in the
corresponding modi®cation solution should be 1 : 1.
After a thorough examination it was found that CoCuHCF and
CoHCF ®lms did not differ only in their redox behavior but also
in other properties, particularly in their pH working range. While
the CoCuHCF ®lm exhibited great stability, i.e., a stable response
after 20 CV scans in solutions with pH from 1 to ca. 9 (HCl or
phosphate buffer containing a constant 0.1 mol=L concentration
of sodium ions), the CoHCF ®lm showed a stable response only
within the pH range of 2 to 6. This is considered quite signi®cant,
especially in terms of measuring environmental and biological
samples with pHs of 7 and more. Furthermore, the CoCuHCF-
modi®ed CFCMEs showed stable (practically the same) CV
response after having been used in experiments for more than 2
months, and also after more than half a year when stored in air,
i.e., exposed to atmospheric oxygen.
3.2. Characterization of CoCuHCF Film and its
Potentiometric Response to Hydrazine
A thorough CV study of the CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME
revealed the possibility of its being exploited as a potentiometric
microsensor for hydrazine, as the oxidation peak potential was
found dependent on hydrazine concentration [26]. The poten-
tiometric response of such a sensor is likely to be closely related
to the pH of a measurement solution. Thus, the effect of pH on
the electromotive force (emf) response to hydrazine (Hyd) was
measured in 0.1 mol=L NaPBS in the pH range of 4.5 to 8.
Figure 3 presents the dependence of emf response of CoCuHCF-
and CoHCF- modi®ed CFCMEs on hydrazine concentration, in
the form of calibration plots of emf versus log CHyd, at various
pHs of the testing solutions. A perhaps clearer way is displayed
in the inset of Figure 3, where this dependence is presented
diagramatically in the form of the slope of calibration plots as a
function of pH. As evident, the slope of emf response to hydra-
zine at CoCuHCF ®lm increases with pH up to 8 which clearly
follows the decrease in the concentration of H ions. However, at
Fig. 2. Cyclic voltammograms of a) a bare CFCME, and of b)
CoCuHCF, c) CoHCF, and d) CuHCF ®lms at CFCMEs; media com-
positions: a) 0.1 mol=L NaNO3 containing 10ÿ4 mol=L K3[Fe(CN)6], b)
(a)  10ÿ4 mol=L Co(NO3)2, d) (a)  10ÿ4 mol=L Cu(Ac)2; number of
CV coatings (b, c, and d): 15; scan rate: 100 mV=s.
Fig. 3. The dependence of emf response to hydrazine at CoCuHCF-
(solid line) and CoHCF- (dashed line) modi®ed CFCMEs on the pH of
the measurement solution; medium: 0.1 mol=L sodium phosphate buffer
at pH a) 4.5, b) 5.3, c) 5.9, d) 7.0, e) 8.0.
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammetric recordings of a CoCuHCF ®lm growth at a
NaNO3  10ÿ4 of K3[Fe(CN)6]  5610ÿ5 of Co(NO3)2  5610ÿ5 of
Cu(Ac)2; scan rate: 100 mV=s; number of cycle: a) 1, b) 5, c) 10, d) 15,
pH 8, the emf response becomes less stable. Therefore, pH 7.0
was chosen as the optimum for the potentiometric determination
of hydrazine at CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCMEs. The dependence
on pH at CoHCF ®lm (Fig. 3, dashed lines) was made in order to
allow a comparison, for the elucidation of the role of copper in
the CoCuHCF ®lm. As can be seen, the response slope at CoHCF
electrode rapidly decreases with increasing pH. No response to
hydrazine is available at pHs higher than 6 because of ®lm
damage. The bene®t of incorporating copper ions into the
CoHCF structure is therefore quite obvious. It enables an
increased sensitivity for the hydrazine at higher pHs, which is a
consequence of the good stability of the CoCuHCF ®lm in
Figure 4 shows the dependence of emf response and its slope
(Inset) to hydrazine at a CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME on the
coverage=thickness of the modi®cation ®lm. The CoCuHCF
coverage unit used here is a CV run, i.e., a full potential scan
cycle in the modi®cation solution under the conditions given in
Section 2. Since the emf is not related to a sensor surface area, the
term coverage=thickness used in this work in fact represents the
amount of active sites deposited onto the substrate carbon ®ber
surface. As evident, the emf sensitivity increases with increasing
coverage up to 25 CV coatings and then it levels off. It may be
concluded that with 25 CV runs a saturation of CoCuHCF active
sites was reached. A further increase in the ®lm thickness does
not improve the sensitivity since the large hydrazine molecules
cannot intercalate into the ®lm [26], but it also does not affect the
emf response as the CoCuHCF ®lm conductivity is high enough.
25 CV runs coating routine was therefore used in microsensor
preparation throughout this work.
To further investigate the role of various components in the
CoCuHCF ®lm from the potentiometric point of view, ®ve dif-
ferent MeHCF ®lms as CFCME coatings, namely the CoCuHCF,
CoHCF, FeHCF, NiHCF, and CuHCF, were used to detect
hydrazine under the same sensor preparation and hydrazine
measurement conditions. The pH 4.5 of the measurement solu-
tion was selected solely due to a lower stability of CoHCF ®lm at
higher pHs. The corresponding emf versus log CHyd plots are
displayed in Figure 5. Two distinct points are immediately evi-
dent: a large difference in emf response slopes between the cobalt
containing (Fig. 5, curves a, b) and the other three ®lms (curves
c±e), and an apparent similarity in the emf response behavior
within both groups of ®lms. Furthermore, the signi®cance of the
®lm activation, in addition to its composition, for the potentio-
metric measurement of hydrazine was also examined. Table 1
lists the effects of applying two different activation modes on the
initial emf, measured in a blank NaPBS, and on emf response
slope for hydrazine, at all ®ve investigated coatings. The best
activation of the ®lms proved to be the cycling of the potential
between 0.0 and 1.0 V for 15 full cycles. Two activation modes
were applied: i) the so called anodic CV activation (ACVA),
where the ®nal potential of the cycling was1.0 V, which left a
®lm in an oxidized state, and ii) the cathodic CV activation
(CCVA), where the ®nal potential was 0.0 V, which left a ®lm in a
reduced state. Results in Table 1 show a remarkable signi®cance
of both the ®lm composition and the mode of activation, for the
potentiometric performance of the investigated MeHCF ®lms.
Concerning the initial emf, an important indicator of the cap-
ability of a potentiometric sensor, the ACVA treated cobalt
containing ®lms show a distinct advantage over the noncobalt
®lms, while the CCVA treated ®lms show relatively little dif-
ference regardless of the composition (Table 1, columns 1 and 2).
Regarding the hydrazine detection, the cobalt containing ®lms
again exhibit a distinct superiority regardless of the activation
mode, although the ACVA mode clearly caused a certain
enhancement of the emf response to hydrazine in all ®ve cases
(Table 1, columns 3 and 4). On the basis of these observations
(Fig. 5 and Table 1), and bearing in mind the pH dependence
(Fig. 3), a few conclusions concerning the CoCuHCF potentio-
metric behavior may be drawn. The presence of cobalt is decisive

Co and Cu Hexacyanoferrate Modi®ed Carbon Fiber Microelectrode
for the considerably increased emf response to hydrazine while
copper mainly contributes to the enhanced chemical stability of
the CoCuHCF ®lm. However, the fact that the oxidative ACVA
treatment of the CoCuHCF or CoHCF ®lms further increases the
emf response, implies the involvement of cobalt(II-III) redox
couple, presumably in combination with the iron(II-III) active
sites, in the emf operation of these coatings. This assumption is in
agreement with the similar observations of some other authors
[19, 27]. Furthermore, it may also be concluded that the poten-
tiometric response of CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME to hydrazine
is based on a redox mechanism.
Additional experiments were performed to explore the role of
CoCuHCF ®lm anodization or cathodization at the optimum pH
conditions. When a CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME was treated by
ACVA and by CCVA, the emf response slopes for hydrazine in a
pH 7.0 NaPBS were ÿ55.2 and ÿ41.2 mV=decade, respectively.
If the polarization at 1.0 V, after completing the ACVA, was
extended for 2 min, the response slope (ÿ55.2 mV) did not
improve. On the other hand, if the potential after completing
CCVA was kept at 0.0 V for 2 min, the slope decreased to
ÿ30.5 mV=decade. This behavior could be explained in terms of
the amount of Co(III), or Co(III) and Fe(III), in the ®lm. By
completing the ACVA procedure, regardless of an extended
anodization, the oxidation of active sites obviously quickly
reached saturation, whereas the ®lm could not be entirely reduced
even after a prolonged cathodization at 0.0 V, following the
CCVA. The experiments proved that for a proper use of
CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME as a potentiometric sensor for
hydrazine, the proposed activation procedure, the ACVA, has to
be applied prior to each set of measurements, and in particular
when going from high to low concentration measurements.
3.3. Analytical Performance of the CoCuHCF
The calibration plot of the emf response versus logarithm of
hydrazine concentration (log CHyd) in pH 7.0 NaPBS, at a
CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME prepared at optimized conditions,
was linear over 3 orders of magnitude from 1.0610ÿ6 to
1.0610ÿ3 mol=L, with the correlation coef®cient of 0.995 and
the slope (over the whole linear region) of ÿ55.2 mV=decade (at
20 C). The limit of detection was found to be 5.0610ÿ7 mol=L,
as evaluated according to the IUPAC recommendation [28]. The
reproducibility of hydrazine measurements at the CoCuHCF
microsensor, considering the complete measurement procedure,
was found to be very good, with the relative standard deviations
of 3.6 %, and 3.1 % (n  10), at hydrazine concentrations of
1610ÿ5, and 1610ÿ3 mol=L, respectively. The short and the
long term stability of the sensor were also found to be very good.
For example, the emf readings from separate measurements at a
hydrazine concentration of 1610ÿ5 mol=L were 182.4, 181.6,
185.0, and 183.5 mV at 5, 20, 40, and 60 min, respectively, after
hydrazine was added to the testing solution. On the other hand,
after a microsensor was stored in air for 2 months, the emf
response slope for hydrazine, obtained under identical condi-
tions, was still ÿ54.3 mV=decade. Hence, nearly no effects of
atmospheric environment (oxygen, room temperature) were
The emf response times at CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCMEs were
measured under the conditions of changing the hydrazine con-

that the 90 % response times in the case of changing the hydra-
zine concentration from 10ÿ5 to 10ÿ4 mol=L were 30 and 60 s
(Fig. 6, A), and in the case of changing the concentration from
10ÿ6 to 10ÿ5 mol=L (Fig. 6, B) they were 35 and 90 s, con-
sidering the microsensors without and with Na®on coating,

respectively. If the concentration of hydrazine was changed only
2 to 3 times, the response time of a naked CoCuHCF-modi®ed
CFCME was always less than 10 s. The average emf drift mea-
sured over a period of 30 min was ÿ2 mV=min. Figure 7 displays
the dependence of emf response at various hydrazine con-
centrations on the temperature of a measurement solution. The
temperature coef®cients obtained were between ÿ2.6 and
ÿ2.9 mV=C from low to high concentration of hydrazine, which
is close to the value of a standard glass electrode for pH mea-
surement, i.e., ca. ÿ0.04 pH=C [29].
The selectivity performance of the proposed microsensor for
hydrazine was thoroughly examined for a number of ionic and
non-ionic species considered as potential interferents, which
possible interfering activity could be based on their blocking,
exchange, coordination or redox interactions with the sensor
material. The CoCuHCF coated microsensor exhibited an
excellent selectivity against the majority of the tested substances.
At hydrazine concentration of 1610ÿ4 mol=L, and under opti-
mum measurement condition (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.0), no effect on
the measured emf resulted from any amount of the following
species: urate, oxalate, ammonia, urea, surfactants (sodium 1-
octane sulfonate, Triton X-100), glucose, sucrose, Co2, Cu2 (at
2ÿ, K, Na, Li, Ca2, and Mg2. No
interference was observed from CNÿ up to 1000-fold excess.
This proves the proposed sensor very suitable for a variety of
applications as no separation procedures should be needed when
encountering the listed species. However, a few of the tested
species such as sul®de, ascorbate, cysteinic and hydroxylamine,
exhibited considerable interference. Obviously, easily oxidizable
and strong coordinating substances can seriously interfere.
Nevertheless, interference caused by the negatively charged
species can be eliminated through an additional coating of the
sensor with a protective Na®on ®lm. This indeed greatly
improved the selectivity against the anionic interferents although
the response times became correspondingly longer (Fig. 6). For
instance, 100-fold excess of ascorbate, cysteinic or sul®de did not
cause any interferences with the measurement of hydrazine at a
Na®on-coated CoCuHCF-modi®ed CFCME. The interference
arising from hydroxylamine could not be eliminated by Na®on
®lm but, actually, NH2OH is not very likely to be present in the
same sample as hydrazine. As expected, the proposed sensor
cannot discriminate between hydrazine and its derivatives (e.g.,
phenylhydrazine, dinitrophenylhydrazine) and, therefore, it is
sensitive to the total hydrazines. Our experiments, employing
either pure argon or pure nitrogen for purging the measurement
solutions, proved that neither dissolved oxygen nor nitrogen had
any in¯uence on the potentiometric detection of hydrazine of any
concentration within the linear range, using the CoCuHCF
The proposed all-solid potentiometric microsensor for hydra-
zine is prepared by a simple cyclic voltammetry deposition of a
cobalt and copper hexacyanoferrate ®lm onto a carbon ®ber
cylinder microelectrode. In a pH 7.0 phosphate buffer, the sensor
has a near Nernstian response slope of ÿ55 mV=decade at 20 C,
thus exhibiting a high sensitivity. The measurement procedure is
simple and fast as no purging and stirring of a measurement
solution is needed owing to the microdimensions of the sensor.
An extensive investigation revealed that cobalt in this mixed
metal hexacyanoferrate coating is responsible for the potentio-
metric behavior, probably based on a redox mechanism, while the
presence of a second transition metal cation, copper, greatly
improves the stability of the ®lm at higher pHs. This is signi®cant
because at higher pHs the CoCuHCF microsensor exhibits an
increase in the emf response slope which makes the sensor
attractive for hydrazine measurement in neutral media. The
selectivity, stability and repeatability of measuring hydrazine are
satisfactory or excellent. An additional Na®on coating increased
the selectivity against some anionic interferents. Since the con-
ductivity of the CoCuHCF ®lm is good, the internal resistance of
the sensor is low which enables the employment of low impe-
dance inexpensive standard instruments. Because of its excellent
potentiometric featuring, high selectivity and small size, as well
as favorable stability and easy handling and maintenance, the
proposed microsensor is considered to be applicable particularly
in some speci®c analytical tasks when microvolume sample or
microlocation direct measurements of hydrazine are required.
